title: External wall insulation: cost and is it worth it?
description: External wall insulation costs roughly £8,000 to £22,000 in the UK. Here is what drives the price, the yearly saving, payback time and the grants that cut the bill.
blurb: What external wall insulation costs in the UK, the yearly saving, the payback time and whether a grant can cover it.
lede: External wall insulation wraps the outside of your home in a layer of insulating board and a fresh weatherproof render. It is the headline fix for the millions of older UK houses built with solid brick or stone walls, the ones with no cavity to fill, where heat pours straight out through the masonry. It works, and on a cold draughty house it can change how the place feels. The catch is the price: this is the most expensive piece of home insulation most people will ever buy, and whether it pays for itself depends heavily on your house, your heating and whether you qualify for help.
short_answer: External wall insulation typically costs between £8,000 and £22,000 in the UK, or roughly £85 to £170 per square metre of wall once scaffolding and render are included. A semi-detached gas-heated home saves around £310 a year and a detached one around £450, so paid for in full the simple payback runs well over twenty years. It only really stacks up financially when a grant covers most of the cost, or when you were going to re-render the walls anyway.

sections.h2: What external wall insulation actually is
sections.html:
Around a third of the UK housing stock has solid walls rather than cavity walls. Solid walls are usually one or two courses of brick or stone with no air gap inside them, so you cannot fill them the way you can with cavity wall insulation. To insulate a solid wall you either line the inside of each room (internal wall insulation, which steals floor space and means redecorating) or you clad the outside. External wall insulation is the outside option.

The job involves fixing insulation boards, usually expanded polystyrene (EPS), extruded polystyrene (XPS) or mineral wool, to the outer face of your walls, then covering them with a reinforced base coat and a decorative weatherproof render or sometimes brick-effect cladding. The finished layer is typically 90mm to 120mm thick, so your walls visibly grow outwards. Done well it transforms a cold solid-wall house and refreshes the look of the building at the same time. Done badly, or without proper detailing around windows, eaves and damp-proof courses, it can trap moisture and cause damp, so the installer matters as much as the product.

If you have a standard post-1920s house with a cavity, you almost certainly want the far cheaper cavity option instead. External wall insulation is for solid-wall homes, and we cover the broader picture in our guide to solid wall insulation.

External wall insulation is priced two ways: per square metre of wall area, and as a total project. As a rough national guide in 2026, expect £85 to £170 per square metre fitted, with the spread driven mostly by the insulation material and the render finish. EPS boards sit at the cheaper end, mineral wool at the dearer end because it costs more and is heavier to handle. Scaffolding, which you nearly always need, is bundled into most quotes but adds a fixed lump whatever the wall area.

Property typeTypical wall areaTypical total cost (fitted)
Mid-terrace (front and rear only)40 to 60 m²£5,000 to £9,000
Semi-detached (three walls)80 to 110 m²£8,000 to £14,000
Detached (four walls)120 to 160 m²£12,000 to £22,000

Fitted-cost ranges from UK installer guides, early 2026. Includes materials, labour, render and scaffolding. Excludes any grant.

A few things push a quote up: more than one storey, awkward access, a complex shape with lots of bays and corners, the need to extend window sills and move downpipes, and any repair to the existing masonry before work starts. Location matters too, with London and the South East commonly 10 to 20 per cent above the national figure. Always get at least three written quotes that spell out the insulation type, its thickness, the render system and whether scaffolding is included.

sections.h2: How much you save, and the payback maths
sections.html:
The Energy Saving Trust puts the yearly saving from solid wall insulation at around £310 for a gas-heated semi-detached house and around £450 for a detached one, based on recent fuel prices. These are estimates for a typical solid-wall home heated by a gas boiler, and your own result depends on how cold the house is now, how warm you keep it and how much wall you are covering.

The reason the saving looks modest next to the cost is that solid walls, while leaky, are only one of several escape routes for heat. Most of a yearly heating bill is gas, charged at 7.33p per kWh under the Ofgem cap for July to September 2026, so even a big cut in kilowatt-hours translates into a moderate number of pounds. Put the figures together and the simple payback on a fully self-funded job is long:

HouseSelf-funded costYearly savingSimple payback
Semi-detached£11,000£310about 35 years
Detached£17,000£450about 38 years

Saving figures: Energy Saving Trust, gas-heated homes. Gas at 7.33p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026.

That is longer than most people stay in a house and longer than the render is guaranteed for. Which is the honest reason the financial case usually rests on something other than energy savings alone: a grant, a re-render you needed anyway, or a heat pump that runs better in a well-insulated home.

Because solid-wall homes are expensive to treat and often house people on lower incomes, external wall insulation is a priority measure for government funding, and a grant can take the cost to zero or close to it. The two routes that have mattered most are the ECO4 scheme and the Warm Homes funding that has succeeded the earlier Great British Insulation Scheme.

ECO4 places an obligation on energy suppliers to fund efficiency upgrades for low-income and vulnerable households, typically those on means-tested benefits living in poorly rated properties (EPC band E, F or G). Under the ECO4 Flex strand, local councils can also refer households below an income threshold even without a qualifying benefit. Solid wall insulation is one of the core measures these schemes pay for, which is why so many external wall jobs are grant-funded rather than paid out of pocket.

Scheme rules, deadlines and eligibility change often, so check the current position and amounts on the official GOV.UK and Ofgem pages before you commit, and treat any installer who promises a guaranteed grant over the phone with caution. We explain the routes in more detail in our guides to the ECO4 scheme and the Great British Insulation Scheme. If you are insulating ahead of a heat pump, the separate Boiler Upgrade Scheme is worth reading too, since a warm home lets a heat pump run at a lower flow temperature and cost less to run.

sections.h2: Is it worth it for your house?
sections.html:
External wall insulation is worth it in three situations, and a hard sell outside them.

It is clearly worth it if a grant covers most or all of the cost. A free or heavily subsidised job on a cold solid-wall house is one of the best comfort upgrades you can get, and the long payback stops mattering when you are not the one paying.

It is worth considering if your render is already failing and needs replacing. The scaffolding and labour to strip and re-render a house are a large part of the bill, so adding insulation at the same time costs far less than doing it as a standalone project later.

It is worth it if you are moving to a heat pump and plan to stay put for many years. A well-insulated solid-wall home lets a heat pump work efficiently, and the comfort gain over a draughty house is real every winter.

If none of those apply, and you are weighing a full-price job purely on energy savings, the numbers rarely justify it on their own. In that case do the cheaper, faster-paying jobs first. Loft insulation and draught-proofing typically pay for themselves in a handful of years rather than decades, and tightening up your heating controls and flow temperature costs almost nothing. Get those done, then revisit walls if a grant becomes available.

External wall insulation costs most UK households between £8,000 and £22,000, or roughly £85 to £170 per square metre fitted, and it cuts a typical gas heating bill by around £310 to £450 a year. On those numbers a self-funded job takes over thirty years to pay back, which is too long to recommend on energy savings alone.

The verdict flips when a grant covers the cost, when you were re-rendering anyway, or when you are insulating to make a heat pump run well in a home you plan to keep. In those cases it is an excellent upgrade that warms the whole house and refreshes the exterior. If you are paying full price purely to save money, do your loft, draughts and heating controls first and treat the walls as a long-term comfort project rather than a quick financial win.

faq.q: How much does external wall insulation cost in the UK?
faq.a: Expect roughly £85 to £170 per square metre of wall fitted, which works out at about £8,000 to £14,000 for a semi-detached house and £12,000 to £22,000 for a detached one in 2026. The figure includes insulation boards, render, labour and scaffolding, but not any grant. Get at least three written quotes, as price varies a lot with the render finish, the number of storeys and your region.

faq.q: How much will external wall insulation save on my energy bills?
faq.a: The Energy Saving Trust estimates around £310 a year for a gas-heated semi-detached home and around £450 for a detached one. Your actual saving depends on how cold and leaky the house is now, how warm you keep it and how much wall area is treated, so treat these as a typical case rather than a promise.

faq.q: Can I get a free grant for external wall insulation?
faq.a: Possibly. Solid wall insulation is a priority measure under schemes such as ECO4, which fund upgrades for lower-income households on means-tested benefits or in poorly rated properties, and some councils can refer households on income grounds. Rules and deadlines change often, so check the current position on GOV.UK and Ofgem rather than relying on an installer's verbal promise.

faq.q: Is external wall or internal wall insulation better?
faq.a: External insulation keeps all your internal floor space, treats the whole wall in one pass and refreshes the outside of the house, but it costs more and may need planning consent in conservation areas. Internal insulation is cheaper and avoids the exterior work, but it shrinks each room slightly, means redecorating and is fiddlier around windows and skirting. External usually wins for comfort and coverage where the budget or a grant allows.

faq.q: Should I do walls before or after the loft and draughts?
faq.a: After. Loft insulation and draught-proofing cost far less and pay back in a few years rather than decades, so do those first. Walls are the most expensive insulation job and only make strong financial sense with a grant, an existing re-render, or a planned heat pump, so leave them until the cheap wins are done.
